## [00:00 - 03:02]

Hey, this is D-Pesh and in this one slide video, I'm going to talk you through the setup and getting the best out of your setup. And then we'll go into separate videos to talk about the next step. Now, if you're new to Facebook, and maybe even if you're not new, Facebook are really, really cracking down on bad players. So people who are contravening their terms of service and things like that. So the one thing that I want you to do is to make sure, number one, you're advertising from a business manager. So if you've got a personal account, you have an account attached to your personal account, head over to business.facebook.com and get that account upgraded to a business manager. Then your next step is to upgrade to a second business manager. So one profile can have at the moment, at least two business managers. Now, a business manager is a container for your ad accounts and many other things as well like pixels and user access and things like that. But a business manager was designed so that you can run more than one ad account generally across one logging. And that's how most people have used them. Now with Facebook banning not just your accounts for malpractice or even just your ads, they're also doing it to business managers as well. And the problem here is, and you know, I'll give you a recent example, someone I spoke to was doing something quite legit on one account, but on another account in the same business manager, he was doing drop shipping and his customer feedback score was really, really poor. And that's a measure of Facebook looking at your customer experience and kind of judging whether you're giving Facebook users a good experience or not. And it was so bad that Facebook shut down his business manager, which meant all the stuff he was testing on dropship had brought down his main business. Now that is a critical, like, absolute terminal problem. So you want to avoid that. So the way to avoid that is have two business managers. One is a backup or you just run your shady stuff or your testing stuff on a different business manager and have your main business manager for stuff you know is making money. So to give an example, I have two business managers I have one for SM commerce, which is my ad agency and a separate one for Zasar, which is my personal project. So my affiliate marketing, my Shopify, my my course, my brand stuff all runs on a separate account so that if anything does happen, because of the niche I'm playing in, for example, selling a course online is one that Facebook are more stringent with on policy. If I do anything that steps the boundaries and it can happen to anyone regardless of your level of experience, I don't impact my agency business. So I have two business managers for that. So do make sure that you are set up for success there as well.

## [03:03 - 06:08]

Now, once you have your business manager set up, so the training calls here, this is a blueprint blueprint will show you how to set up a business manager, how to set up an account. There are all things that you can get for free online. And there'll be links for you to go and do those things. And you can ask me in the in the groups if you need additional help. But this calls is assuming you know how to do the basics. And the basics include googling for some of the stuff here, which is free, etc. So I want to focus you on the stuff that's going to make you money. Now, when I set up an ad account, so let's say for example, I have an ecom store established or not, I'm always making sure that I have backups in place. So generally, one of the safeguards you can have in case something happens to your account is to back up your pixel. Now, what does that mean? When you have one ad account, you'll have one pixel installed. So the pixel is the kind of be all an end or for your account because without pixel, you just have no visibility on what's happening on your website. So the pixel, you'll install it, you'll be able to track events, the value of your transactions, etc. So when you have one ad account, you get one of those pixels. What I recommend is when you set that ad account up, create an ad account that will just be there to hold the pixel. And so what you do is you create the account, you get your pixel, you install that pixel on your site, and then you create a second account. And then what you do is to share that pixel to that second account. Now, what that means is, if your second account gets shut down, for whatever reason, you know, there are innocent reasons that you can kind of fall foul of Facebook's policy, then it means that if that account gets shut down, you still got access to the pixel because it's in account one. So one thing I just want you to bear in mind here is when you have the two accounts set up, you are using one as your main account and one as backup. So actually, let me fire this up in a separate slide so that you get an idea of kind of what I'm talking about. So here, let's say we have half pixel. Now, the way Facebook is set up is that you can share a pixel across multiple accounts right now. So if I have my original account, let's say which is here, let's position that there. And then we have our main ads account here. What that means is if for any reason at all, we do end up with a situation where, you know, what Facebook is not happy with what we're doing, then we have a backup. So let's say that you're running your ads here, and this account gets shut down. Now, if you're in a one to one situation, and your pixel and your account were attached to one and each other, you actually lose everything, you lose your pixel

## [06:08 - 09:09]

and you lose your ads account. So that's what I want you to take away from this is what you're doing is de-risking your approach. And you know, regardless of if you're doing drop shipping, you want to establish brand, you're doing white label, whatever it is, you got to make sure that you're backing up your accounts. So in this situation, you've been running your ad account, everything's been going great, you've driven, you know, you're doing 30, 40, 50,000 a month, and all of a sudden something happens and all of a sudden your account shut down. All that happens is that this link is broken, but that pixel still exists attached to your original account. So then what you do is you fire up a new account, and you share that pixel back across. Now, if you're wondering, well, you know, what's the, what why share the pixel, why not just create a new pixel? The reason is, there's a few reasons. One is far more convenient because your pixel is attached to your website. So if you're thinking about, you know, the impact of pixel on your operations, it's less hassle to have to kind of pick up the pieces and to rework your website. So it means that you can maintain that one to one relationship. But also that pixel is collecting data into your original account. And what it means is if you want to build audiences, you can continue building audiences. So let's say, for example, this, this account was running for six months or even a year or two years. And then all of a sudden it dies. If you're running the pixel on that one account, it means that all of your audience history and everything is gone. But with this method, it means all your history is still kept because it's funneled into your original account. And that means, for example, if you want to create an audience, you've still got that pixel here as well. So at the very least, I'm just making sure that I have two accounts and I can kind of do this. So hopefully that makes sense for you on accounts. Now, the other question I get is I've got general store, should I set up an account per category? Or should I have an account for my general store? My answer to that is, when I when I make a decision on my accounts, there's a few things that come into play. One is regionality. So one of the key reasons why I split up accounts is to do with account time zones. So for example, if let's say, you're running mainly in North America, but you're based in the UK, or in the Far East or something like that, is far better for you to run your account on the on the US time zone. So let's say central central time in the US. The reason is when you're running your optimizations, when Facebook's doing its learning, when it's running its 24 hour cycles. For example, at 12 o'clock at night, everything resets the day resets the 12 to 24 hours starts again. Now, you don't want that

## [09:09 - 12:15]

happening part of the way through the US day, because that can have a detrimental impact. So it's better to set up the account ideally in the area you're operating in. Now, if you're global, then you might want to split your accounts based on those different time zones. So that's generally something I've done as well. In other cases, I've done it by language. So what I've had multi language teams, I've set up an account which is French, for example, so we'll have France, maybe French speaking Switzerland and Belgium, and maybe French speaking Canada. So the time zones are not massively off. So that's just something to bear in mind. And then the other one is, if you have a general store and the audiences are very, very different. So like I'm thinking a real super general store. And let's say you've got an audience of mums and it's all baby wear, then you've got an audience of 45 plus Harley Davidson motorcycle owners, and it's all kind of heavy metal gear jackets and you know, leather belts and all that kind of stuff. Then you may want a different account for those. Even then, I wouldn't necessarily do that. And there's different ways to get the best performance. But it's really down to you on how you want to develop that account. So if for example, like the learning sits at the account level, this is another thing which has been a kind of misunderstanding for a lot of people, which is, where does the learning sit? It's at the account level. So think about where you want that learning to happen. So in most cases with general store, I just have a single pixel. And then I'll let the pixel send the data to my account, and then use that to focus on the right users. Now, if the users, the customers are very, going to be very, very different from my general store, and I want to run broad audiences, then I'm going to make sure that I'm running those in different accounts. So that for example, once I've got enough data, I can actually take the shackles off and let Facebook run a broad audience and bring in the best audience that I can get as well. So there's just a few of the considerations. There's no right or wrong, there's recommended practice and things like that. But my approach would be generally keep it as simple as possible. ## [12:15 - 15:19]

account and just let Facebook take money from you, but get something back, pick up a payment card, which gives you something back. Now, one thing I will mention around this is keep an eye on your account spend limit and also make sure that your card provider are not going to block you for fraudulent transactions. That's a common thing that occurs when it's kind of looking at things like multiple spends in the day because Facebook will be triggering money being taken from your account based on different spend levels, depending on where you are. And it can come across as fraudulent as well. So make sure you do give your bank a heads up and make sure especially when you're ready to scale that you're letting your bank know and you have backup cards ready as well. This plays a big impact on your ads performance. Now Facebook made a number of changes in 2018 and one of them which I've seen is that an engaged page brings down your cost per thousand impressions and thus your cost per click and therefore your cost per acquisition. So don't treat your page as just something you have to do because you're advertising and stuff like that. It actually performs an important part of your advertising strategy. So set up a really good banner, make sure everything is in a good shape on your page. And it looks legit.
